title: process: universal EARS + TDD/RGR acceptance template for all issues
description:
Standardize issue-writing rigor across the codebase so every issue encodes the same quality bar.

EARS requirements:
- When a new issue is filed, the system shall include EARS-style acceptance criteria.
- If behavior is user-visible, then the system shall require unit + BDD coverage expectations.
- Where behavior has edge/failure modes, the system shall require negative, edge, and regression tests in acceptance.

Acceptance:
1) Add a reusable issue template section that mandates EARS + test matrix (happy/negative/edge/regression).
2) Apply template guidance to both new gap issues and retrofit issues.
3) Verify AGENTS/CLAUDE guidance references this universal issue rigor.
4) File follow-up retrofit issues where existing backlog items lack this clarity.

title: trees: malformed query error matrix (parser robustness)
description:
Create an explicit malformed-input matrix to guarantee robust parser failures.

EARS requirements:
- When malformed query input is provided, the system shall return a controlled parse failure.
- If malformed inputs vary in structure, then the system shall produce stable, diagnosable errors.
- Where malformed forms are common user mistakes, the system shall include regression tests.

Acceptance:
1) Add table-driven tests for malformed parens, strings, captures, and predicate clauses.
2) Assert failures are deterministic and non-throwing.
3) Add BDD negative examples for representative malformed queries.
4) Preserve successful parsing behavior for valid inputs.

title: trees/itest: CST-AST query parity regression matrix
description:
Expand cross-tree verification so CST and AST query behavior is intentionally tracked.

EARS requirements:
- When equivalent query intent is run on CST and AST, the system shall meet documented parity expectations.
- If parity intentionally differs by tree shape, then the system shall document and assert that difference.
- Where ordering or capture semantics are observable, the system shall assert deterministic outcomes.

Acceptance:
1) Add parity-focused unit/BDD scenarios across representative query features.
2) Include negative and edge parity checks (not only success counts).
3) Document expected deltas where full parity is not possible.
4) Keep existing CST/AST happy-path scenarios green.

title: trees: define and test duplicate capture semantics
description:
Specify and lock behavior when the same capture name appears multiple times in a query pattern.

EARS requirements:
- When duplicate capture names are used in valid contexts, the system shall apply a documented deterministic rule.
- If duplicate captures are disallowed in certain contexts, then the system shall fail with explicit diagnostics.
- Where duplicate capture behavior influences predicates, the system shall add regression coverage.

Acceptance:
1) Decide and document duplicate-capture semantics (allow/overwrite/collect/reject by context).
2) Add parser/matcher tests for positive, negative, and edge cases.
3) Add BDD scenario(s) that assert the documented behavior end-to-end.
4) Ensure no hidden nondeterminism in capture results.

title: trees: unknown predicate diagnostics with actionable location
description:
Improve unknown predicate handling so failures are explicit and actionable.

EARS requirements:
- When a known predicate is used, the system shall parse and evaluate per current behavior.
- If an unknown predicate name is encountered, then the system shall fail with the predicate name and useful location context.
- Where malformed predicate clauses appear, the system shall distinguish unknown-name from malformed-structure failures.

Acceptance:
1) Add deterministic diagnostics for unknown predicate names.
2) Add tests asserting message includes predicate token and parse context.
3) Add negative BDD scenario proving user-visible failure quality.
4) Keep existing known predicate scenarios green.

title: trees: predicate arity and argument validation hardening
description:
Harden predicate validation for currently supported predicates (#eq?, #match?) across parser and matcher boundaries.

EARS requirements:
- When a predicate has valid arity and argument kinds, the system shall evaluate it consistently.
- If a predicate has invalid arity or argument kinds, then the system shall fail deterministically with clear diagnostics.
- Where predicate behavior differs by argument shape, the system shall cover boundary and regression cases.

Acceptance:
1) Add tests covering valid and invalid argument shapes for #eq? and #match?.
2) Add negative tests for too few/too many args and wrong arg types.
3) Add BDD scenario(s) for semantic predicate failures.
4) Preserve existing successful predicate behavior.

title: itest/query.feature: retrofit EARS-mapped scenarios
description:
Retrofit query BDD scenarios so existing behavior is expressed with EARS clarity and stronger coverage.

EARS requirements:
- When a supported query feature is used, the system shall produce expected captures/matches.
- If query text is invalid or semantically invalid, then the system shall fail with assertable diagnostics.
- Where cross-tree behavior is intended, the system shall assert equivalent/expected CST and AST outcomes.

Acceptance:
1) Expand query.feature with negative and edge scenarios for already-supported features.
2) Map scenarios to EARS-style requirement statements (IDs or explicit clause references).
3) Add at least one regression scenario per major existing feature slice.
4) Keep all pre-existing happy-path scenarios green.

title: itest/QuerySteps: expand and harden step language for advanced assertions
description:
Expand BDD step language where current phrases are insufficient for precise assertions.

EARS requirements:
- When existing steps are insufficient to express behavior, the system shall add composable step phrases.
- If a new step phrase is added, then the system shall verify it with positive and negative scenarios.
- Where diagnostics or ordering matter, the system shall expose assertable step-level hooks.

Acceptance:
1) Add/adjust steps for clearer parse-vs-eval failures and ordering/grouping checks where needed.
2) Validate each new/updated step with positive, negative, and edge scenarios where applicable.
3) Keep backward compatibility for current feature text unless explicitly migrated.
4) Ensure step failure messages remain actionable for debugging.

title: trees/matcher: retrofit semantic negative + edge coverage
description:
Backfill matcher semantics coverage for currently implemented behavior.

EARS requirements:
- When matcher predicates evaluate against valid captures, the system shall preserve current matching semantics.
- If captures are missing/invalid for a predicate, then the system shall fail deterministically without hidden success.
- Where matching order is observable, the system shall preserve and assert deterministic ordering.

Acceptance:
1) Add matcher tests for negative predicate cases, no-text capture behavior, and ordering-sensitive assertions.
2) Add regression tests for multi-pattern and ordered-child semantics already implemented.
3) Include explicit failure assertions, not only count-based happy-path checks.
4) Preserve current valid behavior for existing green scenarios.

title: trees/parser: retrofit negative + edge diagnostics coverage
description:
Backfill parser tests for existing syntax/features with explicit negative and edge coverage.

EARS requirements:
- When malformed query syntax is parsed, the system shall fail with stable, actionable diagnostics.
- If predicate arguments are malformed, then the system shall reject the query with clear failure messaging.
- Where parsing behavior depends on ordering/position, the system shall assert deterministic outcomes.

Acceptance:
1) Add parser tests for malformed parens/strings/captures and invalid predicate argument shapes.
2) Include at least one regression test for each previously ambiguous parser behavior.
3) Include negative tests and edge/boundary tests in addition to happy paths.
4) No runtime exceptions for malformed input; failures are surfaced as parser failures.

title: itest/trees: retrofit EARS + negative/edge/regression rigor across existing query features
description:
Retrofit testing rigor across already-implemented tree query behaviors, not only new gaps.

EARS requirements:
- When an existing query feature is exercised, the system shall keep current happy-path behavior green.
- If a feature currently lacks negative/failure coverage, then the system shall add explicit failing scenarios and assertions.
- Where boundary or edge conditions exist, the system shall add deterministic assertions (including ordering where relevant).

Acceptance:
1) Child issues exist for parser, matcher, query.feature, and QuerySteps retrofit slices.
2) Each child issue includes unit and/or BDD coverage beyond happy path (negative + edge + regression).
3) Existing green scenarios remain green while rigor is added.
4) All child issues are tracked and progressed via strict Red-Green-Refactor.

title: trees: multiple top-level patterns in a single query
description:
Support query strings containing multiple top-level patterns in a single query expression.

Acceptance:
1) Parser accepts query text like `(A) (B)` (plus optional predicates).
2) Matcher evaluates all top-level patterns and returns combined matches in deterministic order.
3) Existing single-pattern queries remain unchanged.
4) Add unit tests and one BDD scenario demonstrating behavior on real CST/AST input.
close_reason: Implemented multi top-level query pattern support with deterministic pattern-order matching and expanded unit/BDD edge-case coverage.

title: trees: validate predicate capture references
description:
Predicate clauses should fail fast when referencing captures that are never bound in the root pattern.

Acceptance:
1) QueryParser returns Failure for queries where a predicate capture (e.g. @missing) is not bound by the root pattern.
2) Error message lists missing capture names.
3) Existing valid queries continue to parse and match unchanged.
4) Add unit tests and one BDD scenario proving this behavior.
close_reason: Implemented strict parser validation for unbound predicate capture references with red-green tests and BDD coverage.

title: trees: Unfielded child patterns and ordered child matching
description:
Add query support for unfielded child patterns and deterministic ordered child matching.

Goal:
- Allow queries like  without field names.
- Match these child patterns against  in order.
- Preserve existing field-based semantics.

Acceptance:
1) Parser accepts unfielded child subpatterns in node patterns.
2) Matcher enforces ordered child matching for those positional patterns.
3) Mixed queries with both field and positional child patterns are either clearly supported or explicitly rejected with a helpful parser error (pick one and test it).
4) Add unit tests and at least one BDD scenario proving behavior on real CST/AST input.
close_reason: Implemented parser+matcher support for unfielded child patterns with ordered matching, added mixed field/child semantics, and covered with unit + BDD tests.

title: parser: multiple top-level value declarations drop to one
description:
Discovered while adding QueryableTree[CstNode] tests. A module source with three adjacent value declarations only produces one CstValueDeclaration in CstModule.declarations; the second and third are silently dropped.

Repro (any of these only yields one value declaration):

  module App exposing (..)

  import Html

  x = 1
  y = 2
  z = 3

Or with blank lines between them:

  module App exposing (..)

  import Html

  x = 1

  y = 2

  z = 3

Expected: CstModule.declarations.size == 3.

Likely in ModuleParser / DeclarationParser — probably the top-level decl loop stops after the first successful decl instead of looping until EOF.

Priority medium — blocks any BDD query test that wants to iterate over a handful of declarations, and is likely to bite real Elm users fast.

Part of the core parser, not the trees work — file under parser.

title: trees: NodeTypeName as a validated neotype
description:
Tighten QueryableTree[T].nodeType so it returns a validated NodeTypeName rather than a raw String.

Use neotype (https://github.com/kitlangton/neotype) for the wrapper. Validation rules:
- Non-null (guaranteed by opaque-type ergonomics).
- Non-empty.
- Not all whitespace.

Touch points when swapping:
- QueryableTree[T].nodeType signature.
- ToyTree's given instance.
- QueryParser: node-type names emerging from the grammar construct NodeTypeName; validation errors point at the offending token.
- Matcher: compare captured node types via NodeTypeName equality.
- BDD steps: query strings are still raw strings; only the internal model hardens.

Scheduling: immediate follow-up after the v1 epic ships. Part of the v2 'tightening restrictions and adding safety' theme.

title: itest: migrate ExpressionSteps to query-based assertions
description:
Validation task that proves the new QuerySteps pack actually simplifies existing tests. Rewrite krueger/itest/src/io/eleven19/krueger/itest/steps/ExpressionSteps.scala using query strings + capture assertions, and rewrite the matching scenarios in krueger/itest/resources/features/expressions.feature to use the new step verbs.

Keep the old step class compiling only if needed to avoid breaking other feature files during migration; otherwise delete it.

Acceptance: expressions.feature still passes end-to-end; new feature file text is at least as readable as the old; lines-of-code in the steps file drops noticeably.

title: itest: QuerySteps BDD step pack
description:
Add a new step class under krueger/itest/src/io/eleven19/krueger/itest/steps/QuerySteps.scala that exposes a generic query API to Gherkin.

Proposed steps:
- When the CST is queried with "<s-expr>"
- When the AST is queried with "<s-expr>"
- Then the query matches exactly {int} times
- Then the query matches at least {int} times
- Then capture {string} of match {int} is a {string}
- Then capture {string} of match {int} has text {string}

State: TestDriver gets a new 'lastMatches: List[Match[CstNode | AstNode]]' field so assertions can refer back. Predicates '#eq?' and '#match?' are available via the default PredicateRegistry.

Acceptance: at least 3 new scenarios in a 'query.feature' file exercise CST and AST paths using real Elm sources.

title: core: given QueryableTree[AstNode]
description:
Mirror of the CstNode instance for the AST side. Same four methods, same dispatch pattern, but over the AST node hierarchy (AstNodes/AstVisitor/AstCursor under krueger/core/src/io/eleven19/krueger/ast/).

Acceptance: symmetric test set to the CST instance — a parsed-to-AST module yields the same shape of matches for the equivalent queries, allowing feature files to assert against either tree.

title: core: given QueryableTree[CstNode]
description:
Provide the QueryableTree instance for CstNode so BDD tests and callers can query real Elm CSTs.

Implementation notes:
- nodeType: use the runtime class's simple name (e.g. 'CstIntLiteral'). Keep as a single match-on-class helper to avoid drift.
- children: delegate to CstVisitor.children (already exists — see krueger/core/src/io/eleven19/krueger/cst/CstVisitor.scala).
- fields: expose named sub-trees per case class — e.g. CstValueDeclaration gets fields 'name' -> Seq(value.name), 'body' -> Seq(value.body), 'patterns' -> value.patterns, 'annotation' -> value.annotation.toList. Write a central dispatch mirroring CstVisitor.children.
- text: None for compound nodes; Some(value) for CstName, CstIntLiteral (stringified), CstFloatLiteral, CstStringLiteral, CstCharLiteral, CstComment.

Lives in krueger.core (which depends on krueger.trees, not the other way around).

Acceptance: query '(CstValueDeclaration name: (CstName) @n)' against a parsed module returns one match per value declaration, with capture 'n' bound to the CstName node.

title: trees: matcher engine with pluggable predicates
description:
Run a Query against any T given a QueryableTree[T] and yield all matches.

API:
- def matches[T: QueryableTree](query: Query, root: T, registry: PredicateRegistry = PredicateRegistry.default): LazyList[Match[T]]
- class PredicateRegistry(predicates: Map[String, PredicateImpl])
- trait PredicateImpl: def evaluate(args: List[PredicateArg], captures: Map[String, Any]): Boolean
- PredicateRegistry.default includes '#eq?' and '#match?'

Algorithm: depth-first traversal over the root, at each node attempt to match the top-level pattern; for a matching node, collect field children via QueryableTree.fields, recursively match sub-patterns; filter candidate matches through predicates; return LazyList so callers can short-circuit.

Acceptance: unit tests over the ToyTree cover node, field, capture, wildcard, #eq?, and #match?; empty-query, no-match, and many-match cases all tested.

title: trees: S-expression query parser (Parsley)
description:
Parse tree-sitter-style query strings into the v1 query AST using Parsley (already a project dependency).

Supported syntax for v1:
- Node pattern: (NodeType)
- Field pattern: (NodeType field_name: (ChildType))
- Capture: @name appended to any pattern, e.g. (NodeType) @decl
- Wildcard: _ and (_)
- Predicate clause at top level: (#eq? @a @b) or (#match? @a "regex")
- Whitespace and line comments (;; ...)

Validation: the parser resolves NodeType strings against a caller-supplied 'known types' set and emits a helpful error if a name is unknown. This keeps runtime errors near the query boundary.

Acceptance: round-trip tests for a canonical set of queries; parser errors point to the offending column; malformed queries return Failure, not exceptions.

title: trees: query AST (Pattern, Capture, Predicate, Match)
description:
Design and implement the data model the parser produces and the matcher consumes.

ADT sketch:
- sealed trait Pattern
- case class NodePattern(nodeType: String, fieldPatterns: List[FieldPattern], capture: Option[String]) extends Pattern
- case class FieldPattern(name: String, pattern: Pattern)
- case class WildcardPattern(capture: Option[String]) extends Pattern   // for tree-sitter '_'
- sealed trait Predicate
- case class EqPredicate(left: PredicateArg, right: PredicateArg) extends Predicate
- case class MatchPredicate(arg: PredicateArg, regex: String) extends Predicate
- case class Query(root: Pattern, predicates: List[Predicate])
- case class Match[T](root: T, captures: Map[String, T])

Keep alternation, quantifiers, anchors, and negation OUT of v1 — document them as TODO comments with links to the v2 epic.

Acceptance: ADT compiles on all platforms; round-trip tests (build AST by hand, pretty-print, re-parse) planned for the parser issue.

title: trees: QueryableTree[T] typeclass
description:
Define the generic typeclass that every tree type implements to participate in the query engine.

Signature (v1, intentionally minimal):

trait QueryableTree[T]:
    def nodeType(t: T): String
    def children(t: T): Seq[T]
    def fields(t: T): Map[String, Seq[T]]
    def text(t: T): Option[String]

Also ship:
- A tiny in-module 'ToyTree' ADT with a QueryableTree given for use in unit/property tests.
- Smoke tests validating the four-method contract and basic invariants (children is stable, fields values appear in children, nodeType is non-empty).

Acceptance: trait lives in package io.eleven19.krueger.trees; ToyTree + tests compile and pass on all three platforms.

title: trees: module scaffolding + cross-platform build
description:
Create new krueger/trees sibling module next to krueger/core. Cross-platform targets: JVM, Scala.js, Scala Native. Wire into build.mill.yaml with matching versions and compiler flags to core. Add a minimal 'package.mill' placeholder and one trivial ZIO Test so CI has something to run.

No Hearth or Kindlings dependencies yet (v1 is hand-written).

Acceptance:
- 'mill krueger.trees.jvm.compile' succeeds.
- 'mill krueger.trees.js.compile' succeeds.
- 'mill krueger.trees.native.compile' succeeds.
- 'mill krueger.trees.jvm.test' runs at least one smoke test.

title: [epic] krueger-trees: generic queryable-tree DSL (v1)
description:
Deliver a tree-sitter-inspired query language over a generic QueryableTree[T] typeclass. v1 is string-based, runtime-validated, with hand-written instances.

Consumers: CST/AST query assertions in BDD tests, plus programmatic node selection.

Scope:
- New krueger.trees cross-platform module (JVM/JS/Native).
- Query AST + Parsley-based S-expression parser.
- Matcher engine with pluggable predicates.
- Given instances for CstNode and AstNode.
- New BDD step pack that uses query strings.

Out of scope for v1 (tracked in the v2 deferred epic):
- Mirror/Hearth-derived instances.
- NodeTypes type member.
- NodeByName match-type narrowing.
- Typed DSL surface.
close_reason: All v1 children delivered: module scaffolding, QueryableTree typeclass, query AST, Parsley parser, matcher engine, CstNode + AstNode given instances, QuerySteps BDD pack, migrated expressions.feature, and docs.

title: Differentiate Elm doc comments from regular comments
description: Capture Elm line comments, block comments, and doc comments distinctly instead of treating every comment only as whitespace. Keep existing parsing behavior while exposing comment kind in parsed CST modules.
close_reason: CST now records line, block, and doc comments distinctly, with unit and BDD coverage.

title: Move Morphir corpus coverage into BDD tests
description: Use finos/morphir-examples and finos/morphir-elm Elm fixtures through the existing Cucumber feature files and TestDriver pattern instead of a standalone unit corpus spec.
close_reason: Moved Morphir corpus coverage into Cucumber feature scenarios using TestDriver resource fixture loading, removed standalone unit corpus spec, and verified JVM unit plus integration tests.

title: Add Morphir Elm parser corpus fixtures
description: Use Elm sources from finos/morphir-examples and finos/morphir-elm as parser fixture coverage and fill parser gaps required by those real examples.
close_reason: Added Morphir corpus parser fixtures, implemented missing syntax needed by those fixtures, formatted sources, and verified all tests.

title: Format test sources for green build
description: Run the project formatter so scalafmt checkFormat passes after annotated value declaration parser work. Verify unit and integration tests before pushing.
close_reason: Ran scalafmt reformat and verified checkFormat, JVM unit tests, and cucumber integration tests pass locally.

title: Parser: support value declarations with type annotations (indentation-sensitive parsing)
description:
Elm's canonical form for an annotated value binding is two consecutive top-level lines:

    foo : Int
    foo = 42

The current Krueger parser cannot parse this. When typeExpression finishes parsing `Int`, its inner `appType = atomType <~> many(atomType)` greedily matches the next `foo` as a lowercase typeVariable (via `lowerName`), consuming the identifier that should have started the next value declaration. The valueDeclaration that follows the annotation then fails because its lowerName has no input.

This is not an atomic-backtracking issue — the type-expression parse genuinely SUCCEEDS with the wrong reading. Fixing it requires indentation-sensitive parsing: the grammar must treat a column-1 (or less-indented-than-type-expr) identifier on a new line as ENDING the type expression, not continuing it.

Scope

Two scenarios were removed from the integration-test suite in the PR that introduced it, pending this work:

- declarations.feature — "Value declaration with annotation"
- lowering.feature — "Lowering surfaces type annotations"

Annotation lowering is still regression-protected via a direct CST unit test
(CstLoweringSpec.scala → "lowerDeclaration carries a value's type annotation onto the AST"), so only the PARSER path is unprotected.

Acceptance criteria

1. `module M exposing (..)\nfoo : Int\nfoo = 42\n` parses via Krueger.parseCst / parseAst without consuming the second `foo` as part of the type expression.
2. The two cucumber scenarios listed above are restored in krueger/itest/resources/features/ and pass.
3. A dedicated ModuleParserSpec test is added that parses an annotated value decl and asserts both the annotation and body on the CstValueDeclaration.
4. No regression in the existing 70 unit tests or 44 cucumber scenarios.

Design options (non-binding)

- Use parsley's pos to snapshot the column at the start of the type expression, and in many(atomType) / many("->" *> appType) bail via lookAhead when the next token's column is ≤ the start column. Matches Elm's offside rule without a full pre-pass.
- Introduce an "indented" combinator utility and thread it through DeclarationParser.typeExpression (and eventually expression parsing). More invasive but pays off for let/case bodies too.
- Pre-tokenisation layout pass that injects `{`, `}`, `;` tokens at block boundaries (Haskell-style). Largest change, most faithful to Elm.

Whichever path is taken, criterion #2 is the ground truth.
close_reason: Implemented indentation-sensitive type-expression tails for annotated value declarations and restored parser/lowering coverage.

title: trees: broaden predicate/directive compatibility surface
description:
Extend predicate/directive compatibility toward tree-sitter parity while maintaining clear unsupported behavior rules.

EARS requirements:
- When supported predicates/directives are used, the system shall parse/evaluate them per documented semantics.
- If unsupported predicate/directive forms are used, then the system shall fail with explicit unsupported diagnostics.
- Where directives affect matching metadata, the system shall keep behavior deterministic and testable.

Acceptance:
1) Define supported vs unsupported predicate/directive set explicitly.
2) Add parser/matcher handling and tests for selected new supported forms.
3) Add negative tests for unsupported forms with stable diagnostics.
4) Add BDD scenarios and step-language extensions where needed for directive assertions.

title: trees: anchor semantics for sibling/positional constraints
description:
Introduce anchor semantics aligned with tree-sitter-style positional constraints.

EARS requirements:
- When anchors are used in valid positions, the system shall enforce the documented positional constraint.
- If anchors are used in invalid contexts, then the system shall fail with actionable diagnostics.
- Where anchor rules affect ordering and adjacency, the system shall assert deterministic outcomes.

Acceptance:
1) Parser validates anchor placement and reports invalid placement failures clearly.
2) Matcher enforces anchor semantics with deterministic ordering behavior.
3) Add positive, negative, edge, and regression tests (unit + BDD).
4) Add/expand step language for ordering/adjacency assertions where required.

title: trees: negated field constraints support (!field)
description:
Add support for negated field constraints in node patterns.

EARS requirements:
- When negated field constraints are used validly, the system shall enforce field absence semantics.
- If negated fields conflict with positive field constraints, then the system shall fail with explicit diagnostics.
- Where optional/empty field representations differ by tree type, the system shall define and test expected behavior.

Acceptance:
1) Parser accepts valid !field forms and rejects invalid/conflicting forms.
2) Matcher enforces negated field semantics with deterministic results.
3) Add positive, negative, edge, and regression tests (unit + BDD).
4) Expand step language where needed for field-absence assertions.

title: trees: alternation patterns support (tree-sitter-style [])
description:
Add tree-sitter-style alternation patterns in the query language.

EARS requirements:
- When alternation syntax is valid, the system shall parse and match any branch deterministically.
- If alternation syntax is malformed, then the system shall fail with clear diagnostics.
- Where alternation interacts with captures/fields, the system shall preserve deterministic capture semantics.

Acceptance:
1) Parser accepts alternation forms and rejects malformed alternation with actionable errors.
2) Matcher evaluates alternation branches correctly with deterministic results.
3) Include unit + BDD positive, negative, and edge tests.
4) Expand step language where needed to express alternation assertions clearly.

title: trees: quantifier support for query patterns (* + ?)
description:
Add quantifier support for query patterns with clear semantics and diagnostics.

EARS requirements:
- When quantifiers are used on supported pattern forms, the system shall match according to documented semantics.
- If quantifiers are applied in unsupported/malformed positions, then the system shall fail with clear diagnostics.
- Where quantifiers create ambiguity, the system shall define and assert deterministic behavior.

Acceptance:
1) Parser supports valid quantifier placements and rejects invalid placements.
2) Matcher behavior for *, +, ? is documented and tested, including boundary cases.
3) Include positive, negative, edge, and regression tests in unit + BDD layers.
4) Expand Gherkin step language where needed for repeated-capture assertions.

title: trees: typed DSL surface with typed captures
description:
Builder DSL on top of the match-type NodeByName so queries can be written in Scala and get full compile-time checking.

Shape:
    val q = query[CstNode]:
        node[CstValueDeclaration]
            .field("name", node[CstName].capture("n"))
            .capture("decl")

Running the query yields Match with typed captures: captures.get["decl"] is CstValueDeclaration, captures.get["n"] is CstName — no casts.

Depends on NodeTypes + NodeByName (filed separately in v2). Part of the v2 epic.

title: trees: Mirror/Hearth-derived QueryableTree instances
description:
Macro-backed auto-derivation so any sealed ADT with a Mirror.SumOf gets a QueryableTree instance with zero hand-written code.

Libraries to evaluate:
- Hearth (https://github.com/MateuszKubuszok/hearth) — cross-compiles JVM/JS/Native, strong macro utilities.
- Kindlings (https://github.com/kubuszok/kindlings) — derivation toolkit on top of Hearth.

Spike plan:
1. Derive NodeTypes union from MirroredElemTypes.
2. Derive resolve() table from MirroredElemLabels + generated TypeTests.
3. Derive fields() by scanning case-class parameter labels + accessors.
4. Decide Hearth-only vs Hearth+Kindlings.

Part of the v2 epic.

title: trees: NodeTypes type member + match-type narrowing
description:
Augment QueryableTree[T] with a type-level closed universe of node types.

trait QueryableTree[T]:
    type NodeTypes <: T
    // v1 methods unchanged …
    def resolve(name: String): Option[TypeTest[T, ? <: NodeTypes]]

Plus a match-type family for compile-time name lookup:
    type NodeByName[Name <: String, T] <: T

so q.node["CstIntLiteral"] resolves to Pattern[CstIntLiteral] in the typed DSL.

Part of the v2 epic (musing-chaum-7e7242-26x). Gate on real consumer feedback from v1.
notes: Reopened: holding off on type-level narrowing changes until we validate they do not add unwanted friction.

title: docs: README + query cookbook for krueger.trees
description:
Public documentation for the v1 query DSL.

Deliverables:
- A new 'Querying trees' section in the top-level README.md linking out to the cookbook.
- New docs file (krueger/trees/README.md or docs/trees.md — pick one and justify in the PR) covering:
  - Motivation and tree-sitter comparison.
  - QueryableTree[T] typeclass usage.
  - Writing queries: node patterns, fields, captures, wildcards, predicates.
  - Examples against CstNode and AstNode.
  - How to add a QueryableTree instance for a new tree type.
  - Pointer to the v2 epic for the roadmap.

Acceptance: every example in the docs is backed by a test so docs can't silently drift.

title: [epic] krueger-trees: advanced type-safety and derivation (v2)
description:
Follow-up work on top of the v1 string-based QueryableTree. Adds compile-time type safety and reduces boilerplate once real usage of v1 reveals which refinements pay off.

Planned components:
- NodeTypes type member: type-level union of all concrete subtypes on the typeclass.
- Mirror/Hearth-backed derived given instances so new ADTs get QueryableTree for free.
- NodeByName[Name, T] match type for narrowing inline-literal queries to concrete subtypes.
- Typed DSL builder with typed captures (Capture[N] instead of Capture[T]).

Gate: do not start until at least two independent consumers of v1 exist and v1 has been used for >=2 sprints of BDD work.
